module_title: AI Ethics and Responsible Use of AI-L
companion_quiz: AI Ethics and Responsible Use of AI-Q

sources.citation: EU HLEG. Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI. 2019.
Obermeyer et al. Dissecting racial bias. Science, 2019.
Jobin et al. Global landscape of AI ethics guidelines. Nature MI, 2019.
Buolamwini & Gebru. Gender Shades. PMLR FAccT, 2018.

learning_objectives: L1: Define AI bias, fairness, transparency, accountability, and privacy.
L1: Identify the seven EU Trustworthy AI requirements.
L1: State the four NIST AI RMF functions: Govern, Map, Measure, Manage.
L2: Explain how historical bias in training data propagates into model outputs.
L2: Compare individual fairness and group fairness as competing definitions.
L3: Analyse a real-world AI system for bias sources and propose mitigations.
L3: Evaluate whether a deployment satisfies EU Trustworthy AI criteria.

concepts.title: Key Terms
concepts.explanation: Bias: systematic unfair discrimination. Fairness: equitable outputs — multiple competing definitions. Transparency: understandable workings. Explainability: human-readable reason for specific decision. Accountability: responsibility assignment. Privacy: right to control personal data.
concepts.worked_example: Gender Shades (2018): lighter-skinned men error ~0.7%, darker-skinned women ~34.7%. Bias: training datasets overrepresented lighter-skinned faces.

concepts.title: Sources of Bias
concepts.explanation: Bias enters at every pipeline stage: historical, representation, measurement, aggregation, evaluation, deployment. Feedback loops amplify bias over time.
concepts.worked_example: Obermeyer et al. (2019): cost proxy underscored Black patients by 26.3 pp. Fix: replace proxy → gap reduced 84%.

concepts.title: Competing Fairness Definitions
concepts.explanation: Individual fairness, demographic parity, equal opportunity, equalised odds. Impossibility theorem: when base rates differ, satisfying all simultaneously is mathematically impossible.
concepts.worked_example: COMPAS (ProPublica 2016): equal overall accuracy but FPR Black 45% vs white 23% — different fairness criteria, both correct.

concepts.title: Seven Requirements
concepts.explanation: (1) Human agency & oversight. (2) Technical robustness & safety. (3) Privacy & data governance. (4) Transparency. (5) Diversity, non-discrimination, fairness. (6) Societal & environmental wellbeing. (7) Accountability. Informed EU AI Act (2024).
concepts.worked_example: Real-time public facial recognition: EU AI Act = unacceptable risk — banned with narrow exceptions.

concepts.title: NIST AI RMF
concepts.explanation: GOVERN: policies & accountability. MAP: context & potential harms. MEASURE: bias, robustness, explainability. MANAGE: prioritise and treat risks.
concepts.worked_example: Hiring algorithm: appoint ethics officer → audit disparate impact → retrain with per-group recall thresholds.

concepts.title: Explainability vs Accuracy
concepts.explanation: Linear regression: high explainability, moderate accuracy. Deep NN: low explainability, highest accuracy. Post-hoc: LIME (local model), SHAP (Shapley values). EU AI Act mandates explainability for high-risk systems.
concepts.worked_example: SHAP loan denial: debt-to-income −0.32, credit score −0.18. Required under ECOA (US) and GDPR Article 22 (EU).
